dialogue.utterance: I'm here to help - what seems to be the issue today?
I've been having difficulty breathing, along with stridorous respirations since around 1 a.m.
Thank you for the details. When did you start to notice your breathing issues being significant this morning?
It's been the same since I went to bed, no improvement. I'm still having stridorous respirations.
Did you wake up due to this or did it come post-waking?
I woke up to the symptoms after about 2 hours of sleep.
Anything else that stands out along with the breathing, like cold sweats or coughing?
No chest pain, no dizziness. Just shortness of breath, some cold sweats, and a non-productive cough.
Are you experiencing any fever or chills along with this?
No fever, no chills.
Does anyone else in your home have similar symptoms or have been ill recently?
No, I've been alone at home all week.
Can you recall any recent travel that might be relevant?
No, I haven't been out recently.
You look alert. Would you please open and close your eyes for me?
(Opens and closes eyes for the examination.)
I know this might seem simple, but please state your full name and today's date.
Daniel, and today is March 31st, 2020.
Can you squeeze my fingers with both hands?
(Squeezes fingers with both hands firmly.)
Based on my findings, I assess you as alert and oriented with a GCS of 15. Do you have trouble speaking or moving?
No, I can speak and move normally.
Let me check your airway. Your airway is open, and I don't see any signs of obstruction.
Okay, no issues.
I'll leave your position as is since it's comfortable and clear.
Our next stop is your breathing rate and effort. Can I listen to your lungs?
Sure, go ahead.
(Listens to lungs with stethoscope and nods.) Your chest moves evenly. Now I'll observe your breathing rate and depth.
Your current breathing rate is 34 breaths per minute. That is on the higher side for an adult.
Stridor is noted, consistent with upper airway involvement.
Oxygen saturation is at 100%, and your skin looks pink and warm with no cyanosis noted.
Let's take your pulse now. Just a quick squeeze of the radial artery. Your pulse is 81 beats per minute and regular.
Any signs of bleeding or changes in your skin condition?
No, I don't think so. I've been taking it easy.
Great, and your blood pressure readings are within range but I will give you a full check now. We'll take it in both arms.
(Takes blood pressure) Blood pressure is 143/74 in your left arm and similar in the right arm.
Your pulse feels strong.
We'll assess your mental status. Can you tell me what the weather is like today and what city you are in?
It's in Richmond, wet and cool.
You are alert and oriented, score of 15. Any memory issues or numbness?
No, been fully aware the whole time.
Given your symptoms, an intravenous line will help monitor and treat hydration if needed. We will start this now.
I'm checking for jugular venous distension and don't find any.
IV line started in your left arm. You might feel a slight pinching from the needle.
Pulse is now measured at 63 beats per minute and is regular.
Your breathing is now at 12 breaths per minute, significantly lower.
Current blood pressure is 147 over 88, and spo2 at 99%.
We'll record a standard electrocardiogram to ensure it's not a cardiac issue. Just four quick leads on the chest.
The ECG shows sinus arrhythmia and is consistent with breathing irregularities.
(Examines skin.) Skin is pink, dry, warm, with normal turgor and no rashes or scratches.
No new rashes or irritation.
Pupils are difficult to examine due to the treatment environment, but no pain or rigidity in the neck is noted. Trachea shows no deviation.
No vision issues or pain in my neck.
I don't see any bleeding or excess discharge reported by the patient.
My throat is fine, no pain or stiffness.
(Pulls up ECG monitoring leads.) Continued breathing sounds are clear on re-auscultation. Good, continuing to monitor for any changes.
We note mild stridor.
Abdomen is symmetrical with no palpable tenderness or masses.
No indication of abnormal urination.
All extremities are warm, dry, and pulses are strong.
Do you have any allergies to drugs, foods, or other substances?
No drug or food allergies.
Any medications you're currently taking or have taken nearby?
No medications since I'm on no prescriptions.
Any medical history that might be relevant?
No, I've had no recent illnesses or major surgery.
What was the last time you had food or drink?
I had some toast and juice this morning.
And finally, can you give me more detail on the events that led you to call 911?
The trouble breathing was unexpected and a bit muffled at the start, then it just got worse.
Based on your symptoms and no other injuries, we are using protocol 3-11 for acute respiratory distress to guide our treatments.
Glucose is zero, so this is consistent with the ePCR note on vitals.
We've placed the 4-lead monitoring system, and it is set on sinus arrhythmia.
The IV allows us to continue monitoring and administer fluids if needed.
We are using a nasal cannula for CO2 monitoring. Comfortable?
Yes, not too intrusive.
Feeling through the mask, our oxygenation methods are good to go.
I'll help you transfer to the stretcher now, Daniel. You seem to be capable but let me assist for safety.
Okay, thanks for the help.
You will be brought to the stretcher on the porch. Due to your breathing, we will secure you to the stretcher first for the transport.
All equipment is on the stretcher ready to go. The mask from earlier goes on now.
Alright Daniel, we will transport you to St. Mary's Hospital for further evaluation. They can handle your case well in emergency care.
You're moving to the stretcher as we speak and secured for transport.
Time to check your pulse again. Now it is at 72 and seems similar to the previous measurement.
Blood pressure is 137 over 84 now with regular pulse and respiratory rate at 28. Good signs so far.
Oxygen saturation is back at near baseline, your pulse oximeter reads at 99%.
IV line is still in place and functioning as a monitoring device.
As we transport, can you report any pain in the chest or elsewhere again?
No pain now. It was only uncomfortable at first.
Once more, we need a brief report on staying consistent - any new issues since I asked you before?
No, it's been the same degree of discomfort and I don't think anything new has come up.
Alright Daniel, we are almost at St. Mary's entrance. Let's get a final physical check before we wheel you in.
I will inform the hospital that you exhibit symptoms requiring a COVID-19 screening.
NP, we have Daniel with difficulty breathing. Vitals have seen improvement; IV placed, ECG in use.
He has no known drug allergies or chronic illnesses. Treated per protocol for respiratory distress. Ready for transfer.
He has no travel history and no recent potential exposures.
On your end, continue monitoring for any worsening signs.
He is going straight to the emergencies - I'll inform them of the findings and procedures obtained during transport.
Just check his equipment readings, all systems are green for release here.
Daniel, you're going to be wheeled into the hospital now. Just keep breathing and follow my voice, everything's under control.
Female attendants are on standby in the mitigation room. We placed him on the stretcher and you can proceed from here.
I think I can walk in now. Just let me grab my cane.
Based on ambulation expressed, we let him do the same and are here for support for now.
No further steps occurring from this team. You all can manage from here; Daniel is responding well and ready.
All documentation and reporting have been clipped to the patient's chart for review.
We've run through these directives and made every move in line with documented procedure.
No complications during our process as per the dashboard. Expect more direct hospital handling.
A zero pain rating, as per patient, and consistent with current vitals. All systems appear normal at transfer.
All intervention and handling is proceeded done in line with instructions from above protocols, and here it ends safely.
Daniel, you're on your own now and in the capable hands of St. Mary's team.
I want to thank you for helping me out. You guys were very professional.
My pleasure, Daniel. You take care and do well in the hospital.
Daniel, if you need anything, just let the hospital staff know. They have us noted for follow-up if needed.
Hospital staff have a file about your symptoms and documentation, so please hand over personal documentation for them to trace.
Care has been formally passed over. Let us know if they call again for details during admission or prior checks.
The patient has the currently marked IV site and monitoring; also, the ETO2 has been added per triage intake guidance.
All transport documents have been marked and passed off so hospital integration procedures remain effective without restating.
I think that's all. Best of luck in the next step, Daniel. You're going to be fine.
